questions.question: 'You are wrong, because you know neither the scriptures nor the power of God. For in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage.' To whom did Jesus say this?
questions.explanation: Jesus said this to the Sadducees, who denied the resurrection and tried to trap Him with a question about a woman who married seven brothers.
questions.hint: The Sadducees only accepted the Torah and rejected the resurrection.

questions.question: 'Let both grow together until the harvest; and at harvest time I will tell the reapers, Gather the weeds first and bind them in bundles to be burned, but gather the wheat into my barn.' This passage is from the parable of the?
questions.topic: New Testament - Parable of the Wheat and Tares
questions.explanation: This is from the parable of the wheat and tares (weeds), where Jesus explains that the harvest represents the end of the age when angels will separate the righteous from the wicked.
questions.hint: The tares were sown by the enemy (the devil).

questions.question: Complete the Beatitude: 'Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall ...'
questions.explanation: Jesus taught, 'Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied' (or filled). This speaks of those who passionately desire God's righteousness.
questions.hint: This is the fourth Beatitude in Matthew 5.

questions.question: The reference in Genesis chapter 6 to the union between the 'Sons of God' and the 'Daughters of Men' explains the origin of what?
questions.explanation: Genesis 6:4 explains that when the sons of God married the daughters of men, they bore children who became the mighty men of renown, the heroes of old.
questions.hint: This event occurred just before God decided to send the flood.

questions.question: 'Two nations in Rebekah's womb' refers to which two nations?
questions.explanation: The two nations in Rebekah's womb were Esau (Edom) and Jacob (Israel). They struggled together before birth, foreshadowing the future conflict between their descendants.
questions.hint: God told Rebekah that the older would serve the younger.

questions.question: Why did Joseph treat his brothers roughly when they first came to Egypt?
options: To assure himself they were not spies
questions.topic: Old Testament - Joseph Tests His Brothers
questions.explanation: Joseph treated his brothers harshly, accusing them of being spies, to test whether they had repented of their sin against him and to see if they would abandon Benjamin as they had abandoned him.
questions.hint: He was testing their character and love for their youngest brother.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes Abraham's reaction to God's promise of a son by Sarah?
questions.explanation: Abraham initially laughed and doubted when God promised that Sarah would bear a son, showing a moment of faithlessness, though he later believed and was credited with righteousness.
questions.hint: He asked God to let Ishmael live before Him instead.

questions.question: 'Mahanaim' is so called because Jacob saw the angels of God as God's what?
questions.explanation: Jacob named the place Mahanaim (meaning 'two camps') because he saw the angels of God as God's army encamped to protect him as he prepared to meet Esau.
questions.hint: This vision gave Jacob confidence before his reunion with Esau.

questions.question: Pharaoh said, 'I have sinned this time; the Lord is in the right, and I and my people are in the wrong.' After which plague did he say this?
questions.explanation: After the plague of hailstorm (the seventh plague), Pharaoh admitted his sin and confessed that the Lord was righteous, but he hardened his heart again once the plague stopped.
questions.hint: This was the first time Pharaoh admitted his sin.

questions.question: The request of Moses that Israel should be allowed to go and worship God was interpreted by Pharaoh to mean what?
questions.explanation: Pharaoh accused the Israelites of being idle and wanting to go to worship because they were lazy, and he increased their workload, saying, 'Let heavier work be laid on the men.'
questions.hint: He made them gather straw for bricks themselves.

questions.question: The Passover lamb was to be kept until the fourteenth day of the first month when the whole assembly was to kill their lambs at what time?
questions.explanation: The Passover lamb was to be slaughtered in the evening (between the evenings), at twilight, on the fourteenth day of the first month.
questions.hint: The blood was to be put on the doorposts and lintel.

questions.question: Where did Pharaoh's horsemen and his army overtake the fleeing Hebrews who were encamped by the sea?
questions.topic: Old Testament - Crossing the Red Sea
questions.explanation: The Israelites encamped at Pihahiroth between Migdol and the sea, opposite Baal-zephon, where Pharaoh's army overtook them before the Red Sea was parted.
questions.hint: This location was strategically chosen by God to display His power.

questions.question: What was the sin of the Israelites that led to the punishment by fiery serpents?
options: Trying to stone Caleb and Joshua
questions.explanation: The Israelites spoke against God and Moses, complaining about the manna and water, so God sent fiery serpents among them as punishment.
questions.hint: They then confessed their sin and asked Moses to pray for them.

questions.question: During the confirmation of the covenant, who was invited by the Lord along with Moses?
questions.explanation: God invited Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and the seventy elders of Israel to come up to Mount Sinai to worship and see the God of Israel.
questions.hint: They saw God and ate and drank in His presence.

questions.question: Who gave this advice: 'Go into the hills, lest the pursuers meet you; and hide yourselves three days, until the pursuers have returned; then afterwards you may go your way'?
questions.topic: Old Testament - Rahab and the Spies
questions.explanation: Rahab the harlot hid the two Israelite spies and advised them to flee to the hills and hide for three days until the king of Jericho's pursuers had returned.
questions.hint: She then helped them escape over the wall with a scarlet cord.

questions.question: When Joshua burned Ai, how did he also put to death the king of Ai?
questions.explanation: After capturing Ai, Joshua hanged the king of Ai on a tree until evening, and then at sunset he commanded his body to be thrown down at the city gate.
questions.hint: A heap of stones was raised over the king's body.

questions.question: In Jotham's parable of the trees, who is Abimelech very much likened to?
questions.explanation: In Jotham's parable, Abimelech was likened to the bramble, a worthless and destructive plant that offered no fruit or protection, only fire that would consume others and itself.
questions.hint: This parable was a curse on Abimelech for killing his 70 brothers.

questions.question: What was the purpose of preserving the twelve stones by Joshua in Gilgal?
options: To found a place of worship
To remind Israel of crossing the Jordan
To indicate the site was a quarry
questions.explanation: Joshua set up twelve stones at Gilgal as a memorial to remind future generations of Israel that God parted the Jordan River so they could cross on dry ground.
questions.hint: When children asked about the stones, parents would tell them the story.

questions.question: As a Nazarite, what was Samson required to avoid?
questions.explanation: As a Nazarite, Samson was required to avoid wine and strong drink, not cut his hair, and avoid contact with dead bodies (unclean things).
questions.hint: He violated all three conditions during his life.

questions.question: Which city was captured by Joshua before the disaster at Ai?
questions.explanation: Joshua captured Jericho first, with the walls falling down miraculously. The disaster at Ai occurred next when Achan's sin caused Israel to be defeated.
questions.hint: The fall of Jericho was a miraculous victory without battle.

questions.question: Who said, 'I have brought Agag the king of Amalek, and I have utterly destroyed the Amalekites'?
questions.explanation: Saul said this to Samuel when he met him at Gilgal, claiming he had obeyed God's command to destroy the Amalekites, though he had actually spared Agag and the best livestock.
questions.hint: Samuel then said, 'Has the Lord as great delight in burnt offerings as in obeying the voice of the Lord?'

questions.question: Who prayed, 'O Lord of hosts, if thou will ... give to thy maidservant a son, then I will give him to the Lord all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head'?
questions.explanation: Hannah prayed this vow at Shiloh, promising to dedicate her son to the Lord as a Nazarite if God would give her a male child. God answered with the birth of Samuel.
questions.hint: Eli the priest thought she was drunk when he saw her praying silently.

questions.question: Why did Eli fall down and die?
options: The behavior of his delinquent children
The capture of the Ark of the Covenant
The death of his two children
questions.explanation: When Eli heard that the Ark of God had been captured by the Philistines, he fell backward off his chair by the gate, broke his neck, and died. The news of his sons' deaths also contributed.
questions.hint: He was 98 years old and heavy.

questions.question: When did Saul fall at once full length upon the ground, filled with fear because of the words of Samuel?
options: When Samuel rebuked Saul for sparing Agag
When Samuel told Saul that God had rejected him and chosen David
When Samuel condemned Saul for offering sacrifice
When Samuel was invoked from the dead
questions.explanation: When Saul had the witch of Endor summon Samuel from the dead, Samuel told Saul that God had torn the kingdom from him and given it to David, and Saul fell full length on the ground in fear.
questions.hint: Saul had not eaten all that day or night.

questions.question: Amnon's incest with Tamar marked the beginning of what?
options: The intrigues for succession to the throne
questions.explanation: Amnon's rape of his half-sister Tamar triggered a chain of tragic events: Absalom killed Amnon, then fled, then later rebelled against David, nearly costing David his throne.
questions.hint: David was angry but did not punish Amnon, which fueled Absalom's anger.

questions.question: As David took to flight from Jerusalem on account of Absalom's conspiracy, of whom was he apprehensive?
questions.explanation: David was most apprehensive about Ahithophel the Gilonite, who had been David's counselor but joined Absalom's conspiracy. Ahithophel's advice was like inquiring of the word of God.
questions.hint: David prayed that God would turn Ahithophel's counsel into foolishness.

questions.question: To whom did God promise to give the kingdom after Solomon's heart turned away from the Lord?
questions.topic: Old Testament - Division of the Kingdom
questions.explanation: God told Solomon that He would tear the kingdom from him and give ten tribes to Jeroboam son of Nebat, leaving only one tribe (Judah) for David's descendants.
questions.hint: This was because Solomon turned to other gods.

questions.question: What did Solomon provide for Hiram in exchange for timber of cedar and cypress?
questions.explanation: Solomon provided Hiram with wheat and oil as food for Hiram's household in exchange for the timber from Lebanon.
questions.hint: Solomon also gave Hiram twenty cities in Galilee later, but Hiram was not pleased with them.

questions.question: The gift of gold to the infant Jesus indicated His what?
questions.topic: New Testament - Gifts of the Magi
questions.explanation: Gold was a gift fit for a king, symbolizing Jesus' royal status as the King of the Jews and the King of Kings.
questions.hint: Frankincense represented His priesthood, and myrrh foreshadowed His death and burial.

questions.question: Why did Herod order the killing of children in Bethlehem?
options: He learned that a king was born
He had been tricked by the wise men
He could not see the new baby
He searched in vain for the wise men
questions.topic: New Testament - Massacre of the Innocents
questions.explanation: Herod ordered the killing of all male children in Bethlehem two years old and under because the wise men had not returned to tell him where the newborn king was, having been warned in a dream.
questions.hint: This fulfilled Jeremiah's prophecy of Rachel weeping for her children.

questions.question: 'And you, O Bethlehem, in the land of Judah, are by no means least among the rulers of Judah; for from you shall come a ruler who will govern my people Israel.' This statement fulfills the prophecy of whom?
questions.explanation: This quotation is from the prophet Micah (Micah 5:2), who prophesied that the Messiah would be born in Bethlehem Ephrathah.
questions.hint: The chief priests and scribes quoted this prophecy to Herod when he asked where the Christ was to be born.

questions.question: According to Mark, what were the disciples forbidden to take for their journey?
questions.topic: New Testament - Commissioning of the Twelve
questions.explanation: Jesus instructed the disciples to take nothing for their journey except a staff: no bread, no bag, no money in their belts, but to wear sandals and not put on two tunics.
questions.hint: They were to depend on the hospitality of those they served.

questions.question: How many days elapsed between Peter's confession and the transfiguration?
questions.explanation: Six days after Peter confessed Jesus as the Christ, Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain where He was transfigured before them.
questions.hint: Luke says 'about eight days after' using inclusive counting.

questions.question: Why did the young man who wanted to know what good deed would qualify him for heaven 'go away sorrowful'?
options: He was uncertain about treasure in heaven
He would realize little money from the sale of his goods
questions.topic: New Testament - The Rich Young Ruler
questions.explanation: The rich young ruler went away sorrowful because he had great possessions and was unwilling to sell them and give to the poor as Jesus instructed.
questions.hint: Jesus said it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.

questions.question: In Matthew's record of the ethical teaching of Jesus (chapters 5-7), the most important divine demand that will enable a man to inherit the kingdom of God is what?
questions.topic: New Testament - Sermon on the Mount
questions.explanation: Jesus taught that not everyone who says 'Lord, Lord' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only those who do the will of His Father who is in heaven.
questions.hint: He compared those who hear His words and do them to a wise man who built his house on rock.

questions.question: In which neighborhood did Peter say, 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God'?
questions.explanation: Peter made this confession in the region of Caesarea Philippi, when Jesus asked His disciples who they said He was.
questions.hint: This location was north of Galilee, near Mount Hermon.

questions.question: To which temptation did Jesus give the reply, 'You shall not tempt the Lord your God'?
questions.explanation: Jesus said this to Satan when Satan tempted Him to throw Himself down from the pinnacle of the temple, quoting Scripture to test God's protection.
questions.hint: This was the second temptation in Matthew's account.

questions.question: Which parable teaches about judgment and separation?
questions.topic: New Testament - Parable of the Dragnet
questions.explanation: The parable of the dragnet teaches that at the end of the age, angels will separate the wicked from the righteous, just as fishermen separate good fish from bad.
questions.hint: The angels will throw the wicked into the furnace of fire.

questions.question: What does Jesus' saying, 'If anyone would be first, he must be last of all and servant of all,' teach?
questions.explanation: This saying teaches that true greatness in God's kingdom is measured by humility and service, not by power or position, as Jesus Himself came to serve, not to be served.
questions.hint: Jesus used a child as an example of humility.

questions.question: The appearance of Elijah and Moses at the transfiguration indicates the significance of what?
questions.explanation: Moses represented the Law, and Elijah represented the Prophets. Their appearance showed that Jesus is the fulfillment of both the Law and the Prophets.
questions.hint: They spoke with Jesus about His 'departure' (death) in Jerusalem.

questions.question: One of the illustrations of Jesus on true greatness is what?
questions.explanation: Jesus used a child as an illustration of true greatness, teaching that whoever humbles himself like a child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven.
questions.hint: Children in that culture had no status or power.

questions.question: From which town did Jesus send two of His disciples to make adequate preparation for the last entry into Jerusalem?
questions.explanation: Jesus sent two disciples from Bethphage, near the Mount of Olives, to fetch a donkey and a colt for His triumphal entry into Jerusalem.
questions.hint: Bethphage means 'house of unripe figs' in Hebrew.

questions.question: Which religious groups asked Jesus about payment of taxes to Caesar?
questions.explanation: The Pharisees and Herodians together asked Jesus about paying taxes to Caesar, attempting to trap Him either with the Jews (if He said yes) or with Rome (if He said no).
questions.hint: Jesus asked for a denarius and said, 'Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's.'

questions.question: When the Pharisees accused Jesus' disciples of plucking heads of grain on the Sabbath, to what David did during the high priesthood of whom did Jesus refer?
questions.explanation: Jesus referred to David eating the bread of the Presence when he was hungry, which was only lawful for priests to eat, during the time of Abiathar the high priest.
questions.hint: Mark's Gospel specifically mentions Abiathar as high priest.

questions.question: What did Jesus mean when He said, 'Take heed and beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees'?
questions.topic: New Testament - Leaven of the Pharisees
questions.explanation: The disciples understood that Jesus was not speaking about literal bread but about the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees, which was hypocritical and legalistic.
questions.hint: Luke 12:1 specifies that the leaven of the Pharisees is hypocrisy.

questions.question: The three thousand converts on the day of Pentecost devoted themselves to what?
options: Breaking of bread and the prayers
questions.explanation: The new believers devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching, fellowship, the breaking of bread (communion), and prayer.
questions.hint: They also sold possessions and shared everything in common.

questions.question: Two of the seven deacons were?
questions.explanation: The seven deacons chosen were Stephen, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolas. Stephen and Philip are the most well-known.
questions.hint: Stephen became the first Christian martyr.

questions.question: According to Peter's speech before the Sanhedrin, to whom is the Holy Spirit given by God?
questions.topic: New Testament - Peter Before the Sanhedrin
questions.explanation: Peter declared that God gives the Holy Spirit to those who obey Him, emphasizing that obedience is the condition for receiving the promised gift.
questions.hint: He said, 'We are witnesses to these things, and so is the Holy Spirit.'

questions.question: In his speech concerning the replacement of Judas Iscariot, where did Peter quote from?
questions.explanation: Peter quoted from the Psalms, specifically Psalm 69:25 ('Let his habitation become desolate') and Psalm 109:8 ('His office let another take').
questions.hint: The passage from Psalms was seen as prophetic about Judas.

questions.question: When Peter escaped from prison during the Passover, what was the condition of the guards?
questions.topic: New Testament - Peter's Escape from Prison
questions.explanation: The guards were fast asleep when the angel woke Peter and led him out of the prison. The next morning, there was a great disturbance among the soldiers.
questions.hint: Herod had the guards executed for failing to keep Peter.

questions.question: One of the seven Hellenists who preached the gospel in Samaria, and from Azotus to Caesarea, was whom?
questions.explanation: Philip, one of the seven deacons, preached in Samaria (where Simon the magician believed) and then was led by the Spirit to evangelize along the road from Azotus to Caesarea.
questions.hint: He is called Philip the evangelist to distinguish him from Philip the apostle.

questions.question: Why was Simon the magician rebuked by Peter in Samaria?
options: He perverted the minds of the people
He opposed the teaching of the apostles
He wanted to purchase spiritual power with money
questions.explanation: Simon offered the apostles money, saying, 'Give me this power also, that anyone on whom I lay my hands may receive the Holy Spirit.' Peter rebuked him sharply.
questions.hint: Peter told him, 'Your silver perish with you, because you thought you could obtain the gift of God with money.'

questions.question: Where were the disciples first called Christians?
questions.topic: New Testament - Origin of the Term 'Christian'
questions.explanation: The disciples were first called Christians in Antioch, where Barnabas and Saul taught a large number of people for a whole year.
questions.hint: The term was likely originally used as a nickname by outsiders.
